positive.comment: That’s a good question actually! I wanted to go with real phone calls because the fake call screen trick doesn’t always hold up in real situations — people can notice the screen or timing feels off. A real call gives you that authentic ring tone, vibration, and interaction with your carrier’s call UI — it just feels real. Plus, since it’s an actual voice line, you can pick up, talk, and it sounds completely natural to anyone nearby. So yeah, the goal was to make it feel believable, not staged.
positive.parent: One query: why keep a phone call, instead you could keep fake call screen with same voice overs

positive.comment: If anyone wants to try the app. Here is thedownloadlink. It’s free also share your thought here on how was your phone call experience!
It is so true! Thats why I needed this app as well! Please give it a try and also spread the word
positive.parent: For me, it’s the perfect tool to simulate fake calls—like when I’m in a meeting and need a smooth way to leave, or if I know I’ll have to step out after 5 or 10 minutes for something important. It really acts as a kind of “call breaker” for awkward moments or when I need an excuse to exit gracefully. Thanks for creating something so practical!

positive.comment: No it was the issue with ui. Actually the app is designed to be used only on phones. But the app was allowed to install on iPad. Also point to note that they do install on iPad and check thoroughly. So if someone planning to release iPhone only app then make sure you disable iPad to false in the plist. The other reason is the payment wasn’t integrated properly with revenue cat. After fixing both issues finally they approved the release.
